input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a laboratory incubator whose purpose is to bring an insulated container to a target temperature and maintain it independent of the contents. The hardware consists of a styrofoam insulated box, a heating element (heat‑bed), a fan for air circulation, two temperature sensors inside the box and one sensor outside, all supervised by a Raspberry Pi controller. The operational domain is temperature regulation for incubation processes, and the system’s simplicity yet inclusion of cyber‑physical components (sensors, actuator, embedded controller) makes it a representative CPS for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (i) continuous monitoring of the three temperature sensors to detect deviations from the desired temperature, (ii) calibration algorithms that update the thermal model parameters using the latest sensor data, (iii) simulation/prediction services that reproduce and forecast the incubator’s thermal behaviour under current operating conditions, (iv) optimisation services that suggest control set‑points for the heat‑bed and fan to keep the temperature within safe and optimal bounds, and (v) a safety‑checking monitor that alerts human operators or supervisory algorithms when violations are detected, allowing them to intervene on the Physical Twin. Visualization of temperature trends may also be offered.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation leverages the Raspberry Pi embedded platform as the cyber controller, interfaced with the temperature sensors and heating/fan actuators. All hardware and software specifications are published in an open‑source GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator), which serves as the primary enabler for reproducible development. The DT integrates data acquisition, calibrated physical models, and algorithmic components (calibration algorithms, monitors, optimisers) as described in the DT framework. While no specific simulation engine or database is named, the architecture follows the generic DT pattern of combining data, models, and algorithms to generate knowledge and support the services listed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is orchestrated as a layered framework that integrates three core artefacts: (1) **Data** obtained continuously from the Physical Twin (PT) via its sensors; (2) **Models** that represent the cyber‑physical behavior of the PT and its environment; and (3) **Algorithms** that consume the data and models to generate knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators, optimisers). Calibration algorithms are employed to periodically update the parameters of the CPS and environment models using the latest sensor data, thereby keeping the virtual representation aligned with the PT. The calibrated models feed monitoring components that assess safety and optimality criteria in real time. When a monitor signals a violation, a decision layer—implemented by either human operators or autonomous algorithms—triggers corrective actions on the PT. This orchestration follows the schematic depicted in Figure 1 of Document 1, emphasizing tight coupling between sensing, model updating, monitoring, and control loops.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator digital twin incorporates two analytical simulation models of the temperature dynamics. Both models are behavioral thermal models that predict internal temperature; the first model contains two free parameters, the second contains four free parameters to capture the thermal inertia of the heat‑bed. Model data are supplied by temperature measurements from sensors on the physical twin, collected over time. The models reside in the cyber layer and are calibrated by algorithms that adjust the free parameters using the sensed data. The twin also includes the communication software that transports sensor data to the cyber side and makes the calibrated models available to monitoring and optimisation components.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical twin contains a heat‑bed that functions as the heating actuator for temperature control. The heat‑bed is driven by the incubator controller, which receives set‑points from the cyber side via the communication software and can be actuated remotely. The actuator provides heating power within the range required to raise the internal temperature of the insulated box; precise power limits are not specified. Safety constraints are implied by the monitoring algorithms that detect violations and can command the actuator to stop or reduce power.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is realized with two DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire digital thermometers placed inside the insulated enclosure; each sensor reports temperature with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C and transmits its readings over the 1‑Wire bus to the Raspberry Pi, which forwards the data through the RabbitMQ channel for storage and real‑time use by the DT.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by comparing the two thermal models against experimental temperature data. Validation experiments showed that the four‑parameter model more accurately reproduces the thermal inertia of the heat‑bed, thereby offering higher fidelity and being selected for further digital‑twin development. Uncertainty is reduced through calibration algorithms that estimate the best‑fit parameters, and model performance is monitored by the DT’s supervisory algorithms that detect deviations between predicted and measured temperatures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The incubator Physical Twin provides temperature measurements from three sensors (two inside the insulated box, one outside) that are connected to the Raspberry Pi via a 1‑Wire bus. The Pi reads the sensor values and forwards them to the Digital Twin through a RabbitMQ server deployed on the same Raspberry Pi, which acts as the synchronization and communication backbone. Data consists of scalar temperature readings (°C) sampled at the sensor’s native rate (exact frequency not specified). The messages are published on RabbitMQ queues used by calibration algorithms, which preprocess the raw values (e.g., filtering) before updating the cyber models and feeding monitor components. No explicit event‑trigger or quality‑assurance mechanisms are described beyond the use of the calibration step.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The communication infrastructure consists of a single Raspberry Pi that hosts both the controller and a RabbitMQ server. Sensor data travel from the 1‑Wire bus to the low‑level driver, and all inter‑component messages (sensor updates and control commands) are exchanged via the RabbitMQ server. No additional network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols are specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin’s runtime environment is hosted directly on the edge device—the Raspberry Pi that also controls the incubator. The RabbitMQ server and the calibration/monitoring algorithms execute on this platform, implying modest compute and storage needs fitting the Pi’s resources. Scalability is limited to the single‑node deployment; no cloud or multi‑node scaling mechanisms are described. Availability relies on the Raspberry Pi remaining powered; no explicit high‑availability or fault‑tolerance strategies are provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT for the incubator is engineered by first constructing two separate models of the plant and applying a calibration method to align model parameters with sensor data. The models and the accompanying hardware/software specifications are stored in a publicly accessible GitHub repository, providing version‑controlled artefacts. After model calibration, experimental campaigns are executed to validate the models and to collect performance data, which informs subsequent refinements and the definition of future development goals.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital twin runs predictive simulations that reproduce and forecast the physical twin’s thermal behaviour under current operating conditions. Monitoring algorithms continuously evaluate sensor streams to detect safety or optimality violations. When a violation is identified, the twin raises an alert for a human operator (or an automated supervisory controller) who can decide to adjust the physical system. The insight generation relies on the combination of data, calibrated models, and algorithms, though the documents do not specify explicit confidence metrics for the recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The DT architecture described in Document 1 integrates safety monitoring by employing calibration algorithms that update models of the cyber‑physical system and its environment, and then feeds the resulting data to monitors that continuously evaluate whether the system is operating safely and optimally. Violations detected by these monitors can trigger human operators or automated algorithms to initiate corrective actions on the physical twin. While the documents do not detail specific cybersecurity protocols, access‑control, or authentication mechanisms, the safety‑oriented components include the use of runtime monitors and fault‑detectors as part of the algorithmic layer that support risk mitigation and fail‑safe responses.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly accurate and detailed description of the physical system, perfectly matching the hardware specifications and goals described in the source. It correctly identifies the components (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensors, Raspberry Pi), the operational goal (reaching and maintaining temperature), and the context of the system as a representative CPS. Quotes: 'The plant of the Incubator consists of... A styrofoam box... A heatbed... A fan... Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box. One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.'; 'The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and regulate it regardless of the content inside.'; 'simple, yet representative, CPS'.

The extraction correctly identifies services defined in the text's DT framework: monitoring, calibration, simulation/prediction, and safety checking. It accurately reflects the text's statement that the DT provides the ability to 'reproduce and predict' behaviour and employs 'calibration algorithms'. The mention of 'optimisation services that suggest control set-points' is a reasonable but slightly inferred capability from the text's general description of optimisation technologies and human intervention, rather than an explicit operational service detailed in the implementation. Visualization is supported as a potential value-add.

The extraction accurately identifies the key hardware (Raspberry Pi) and the open-source repository as enablers. It correctly summarizes the architecture components (data, models, algorithms) and notes that specific simulation engines or databases are not named. While it omits the specific mention of RabbitMQ (communication server) and the Python Scipy package (calibration), it remains largely accurate and specific regarding the provided tools and framework. Quotes: 'Raspberry Pi 4 Model B was selected to implement the controller.'; 'publicly available repository... contains the up-to-date hardware and software specifications'; 'Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch how such implementation can be done'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions that the low-level driver 'periodically reads the temperature data' and that RabbitMQ is used for 'synchronization and communication'. However, it does not specify synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined in the characteristic description. The extraction 'Not in Document' is technically accurate because the specific temporal aspects are not detailed.

The document describes a single digital twin system for a single incubator. There is no mention of multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. The extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate as no relevant information is present.

The extracted description is a precise and detailed summary of the Digital Twin architecture provided in Section 1. It correctly identifies the three core artefacts: 'Data – collected from the PT through sensors over time', 'Models – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics', and 'Algorithms – techniques that use data and models'. It also accurately captures the orchestration loop: 'the DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models... used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'.

The document mentions the use of RabbitMQ for communication within the system but does not discuss integration with external systems, interoperability standards, or dependencies on external systems. The extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate as no relevant information is present.

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the two simulation models, their parameter counts (two and four), and the purpose of predicting thermal inertia. It correctly describes the data source (sensors over time), the calibration algorithms, and the role of communication software. It is highly specific and well-grounded in the text. Quotes: 'one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters', 'collected from the PT through sensors over time', 'non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate'.

The description correctly identifies the heat-bed as the heating actuator, its control by the Raspberry Pi/controller, and the use of communication commands. It accurately notes the safety protocols and the lack of precise power limits (only 'at least 100W' is given). However, it omits the fan, which is also a physical acting component mentioned in the source. Quotes: 'A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box', 'actuate the heatbed, the fan', 'safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver'.

Detailed and accurate, covering sensor type (DS18S20), quantity, accuracy, 1-Wire bus transmission, and data forwarding to the RabbitMQ server. Supported by quotes: 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box', 'accuracy of 0.5 °C', and 'Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive summary of model fidelity, comparing the two models and identifying the four-parameter model as superior for capturing thermal inertia. It accurately links calibration to uncertainty reduction and mentions the supervisory algorithms for monitoring deviations. Quotes: 'experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia', 'non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters', 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and accurate based on the provided text. It correctly identifies the number and location of sensors (three total: two inside, one outside), the connection method (1-Wire bus to Raspberry Pi), the role of RabbitMQ for communication, and the use of calibration algorithms. It accurately notes the absence of specific frequencies or quality assurance mechanisms beyond calibration. Supporting quotes: 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box. One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box', 'The low-level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are commands from the controller, through the communication server', and 'For the case study, the RabbitMQ server was deployed for synchronisation and communication.'

The document describes the Digital Twin implementation as a component that listens to messages passed between the controller and the low-level driver ('DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed between the controller and the load level driver'). It does not explicitly describe control flows or commands sent from the Digital Twin directly to the physical twin in the provided text. Therefore, stating 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extraction provides a precise summary of the communication infrastructure as described in the text. It correctly identifies the Raspberry Pi as the host for both the controller and the RabbitMQ server, the 1-Wire bus for sensor data, and RabbitMQ for inter-component messaging. It also correctly identifies the absence of specific network topology details like bandwidth or security protocols in the source. Quotes: 'RabbitMQ server, running on the raspberry pi itself.', 'allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus.', 'messages being passed between the controller and the load level driver'.

The extraction is well-supported and specific. It correctly identifies the edge hosting platform (Raspberry Pi), the co-location of the communication server and controller, and the implied limitations on scalability and availability due to the single-node setup. Quotes: 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B... was selected to implement the controller' and 'We used a Rabbit MQ server, running on the raspberry pi itself.'

block_5_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
